title: Pulp painting on wet sheets
subtitle: Applying coloured paper pulp to a freshly formed wet sheet before pressing
excerpt: Pulp painting is the technique of spooning or piping small amounts of differently coloured pulp onto a wet sheet that has just been couched. The applied pulp bonds during pressing and drying, becoming part of the sheet rather than a surface coating. The result is an image or pattern that is integral to the paper and cannot be rubbed off.
sourceNotes: Pulp painting as an artistic technique was developed and documented in the 1970s and 1980s by hand papermakers including Winifred Lutz and Helen Hiebert. Helen Hiebert, The Papermaker's Companion (2000), chapter on pulp painting and formed sheets. The core technique is a synthesis of well-established hand papermaking practice.

content.text: , colour is embedded in the sheet rather than applied to its surface. Coloured pulp applied to a wet

before pressing bonds as the fibres interlock under pressure. The range of marks includes simple geometric shapes applied by spoon, fine lines piped from a squeeze bottle, and layered areas of overlapping colour. The key constraint is that the base sheet must still be fully wet when colour is applied: dried or pressed sheets will not bond with new pulp.

items.name: Base sheet pulp (white cotton linter or abaca), beaten and ready in the vat
Coloured pulp (cotton linter or abaca dyed with fabric dye or acrylic pigment before beating)
Squeeze bottles with fine tips (for controlled line application)
Small spoons or turkey baster (for broader colour areas)
Syringe or pipette (optional, for very fine lines)

content.text: Prepare small separate batches of coloured pulp. Use the same base fibre (cotton linter or abaca) for all colours to keep the shrinkage rate consistent across the sheet during drying. Dye the pulp before beating by soaking the dry fibre in a fibre-reactive dye solution, rinsing thoroughly, then beating normally.

Drain each batch of coloured pulp to a consistency thick enough to hold its shape when dropped from a spoon: denser than vat pulp but not so thick it clogs the squeeze bottle tip. Store each colour in a labelled container.
Form and couch the base sheet as normal. Leave it on the couching cloth, fully wet.

Apply the coloured pulp to the surface of the wet sheet. For broad areas, spoon or ladle the pulp and spread gently with the back of the spoon. For lines and fine work, pipe through a squeeze bottle. Work from the lightest colour to the darkest to avoid cross-contamination of the containers.

Blot excess water from the applied areas by pressing a piece of dry couching cloth gently on top of the coloured pulp for a few seconds, then lifting straight up. This removes surface water without smearing the design.
Press and dry the sheet by any method. The design will be permanently bonded. If board drying, lay the sheet pulp-side up on the board to avoid the coloured pulp facing the board surface.

attrs.intro: Problems in adhesion, colour bleeding, and edge definition.

items.fix: Apply coloured pulp only to a fully wet base sheet. Thin the coloured pulp slightly with water before application.
items.cause: Base sheet too dry when pulp was applied, or applied pulp too thick relative to the base sheet.
items.symptom: Applied colour lifts off the base sheet after drying

items.fix: Leave a small gap between colour areas. Blot between applications to reduce surface water.
items.cause: Adjacent colours applied too close while very wet; capillary action draws the pulp together.

attrs.heading: Common problems with pulp painting
